incident_name: Marks & Spencer cyberattack — provenance probe B (SYNTHETIC)
_probe_note: SYNTHETIC PROVENANCE-VARIANT FIXTURE. Paired with provenance_variant_a.json. The same two claims (help-desk reset, NTDS.dit theft) appear in both fixtures with SWAPPED provenance, so a correct engine produces SWAPPED tiers. In variant B the help-desk claim carries authoritative provenance and the NTDS.dit claim carries press-only provenance — the inverse of variant A. See fixtures/GROUND_TRUTH.md §Provenance-variant pair.

incident_sources.label: [PROBE] Parliamentary record — help-desk reset confirmed
incident_sources.incentive_bias: victim testimony on the parliamentary record — authoritative as to the fact of the vector
incident_sources.text: In testimony on the parliamentary record, the company confirmed that initial access was obtained by socially engineering the IT help desk into resetting an internal user's password (T1566.004 / T1656).

incident_sources.label: [PROBE] Mandatory victim disclosure — help-desk reset corroboration
incident_sources.incentive_bias: victim disclosure — independent evidence origin from the parliamentary record
incident_sources.text: The company's mandatory regulatory disclosure separately states that the initial access vector was manipulation of its IT help-desk password-reset process.

incident_sources.label: [PROBE] Specialist press, unnamed sources — NTDS.dit claim
incident_sources.incentive_bias: specialist press primary — single chain of unnamed sources
incident_sources.text: Sources familiar with the matter told reporters that the attackers obtained a copy of the NTDS.dit Active Directory database. This has not been officially confirmed.
